title: Here's how I use LLMs to help me write code
description: Simon Willison shares his practical experience using LLMs for coding development work. He discusses how using LLMs to write code is difficult and unintuitive, requiring significant effort to understand their limitations and effective usage patterns. The article covers the sharp and soft edges of AI-assisted coding and provides insights into his workflow with LLM coding tools.
author: Simon Willison

title: Adding AI-generated descriptions to my tools collection
description: Simon Willison describes his tools.simonwillison.net site featuring 78 HTML+JavaScript tools built with AI assistance. He discusses the process of adding AI-generated descriptions to his tool collection and shares insights about building tools with AI help, including commit information and development workflow.
author: Simon Willison

title: AI Will Be Writing 90% of Code in 3 to 6 Months
description: Reddit discussion about the Anthropic CEO's prediction about AI-generated code. Professional developers in the comments note that more than 90% of their code is already AI-generated. The discussion covers the distinction between AI-assisted coding and 'vibe coding' - iterative programming with LLMs using natural language. Community consensus that AI is fundamentally changing software development workflows.
author: Reddit r/artificial Community

title: Best AI for Coding? Community Consensus
description: Reddit discussion where the community consensus identifies Claude Sonnet 3.5 as currently the best LLM coding model. Discussion covers tools like CoPilot, Cursor, Cline, and Roo. Participants share practical advice on how to use these AI coding assistants effectively and compare different models for coding tasks.
author: Reddit r/ChatGPTCoding Community

title: Karpathy on RLVR and 2025 LLM Developments
description: Andrej Karpathy shares insights about how Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) emerged in 2025 as a major new development stage for LLMs. He discusses training AI on problems with objectively correct answers and shares 'random notes from coding quite a bit' about his workflow with the latest LLM improvements. Karpathy also made waves admitting he's 'never felt this much behind as a programmer,' highlighting how rapidly AI is reshaping programming.
author: Andrej Karpathy

title: Are LLM merge rates not getting better?
description: A detailed Hacker News discussion from March 10, 2026 exploring whether LLM coding quality has plateaued. Key insights: 1) Models improved dramatically in 2025 with better context management and terminal-based apps like Claude Code, but code quality remains problematic with quadruple-nested control flow and unsound architecture. 2) Developers debate whether LLMs are actually getting better or if improvements come from better tooling and 'harness engineering' rather than intrinsic model improvements. 3) Discussion of 'emergent abilities mirage' - metrics may not capture real-world progress. 4) Consensus that Opus 4.5/4.6 represented a significant step change, but newer models show diminishing returns.
author: Hacker News Community

title: If AI writes code, should the session be part of the commit?
description: Hacker News discussion from March 9, 2026 about version control practices for AI-generated code. Key questions: Should AI coding sessions be recorded in commits? How do we attribute AI contributions? Should prompt context be included in commit metadata? Discussion explores the implications for code review, attribution, legal compliance, and understanding how code was generated. As AI writes more code, traditional version control practices may need updating.
author: Hacker News Community

title: You need to rewrite your CLI for AI agents
description: A March 8, 2026 Hacker News discussion (136 comments) about adapting command-line interfaces for AI agents. As AI coding tools become more prevalent, traditional CLIs need redesign to be more agent-friendly. Discussion covers topics like structured output, better error handling, state management, and designing interfaces that both humans and AI agents can use effectively. This reflects the growing realization that software tooling needs to evolve for the AI-agentic era.
author: Hacker News Community
